ideas.Tradition-constituted rationality:
"**Tradition-constituted rationality**" is a central and distinctive thesis of Alasdair MacIntyre's mature philosophy — his claim that **rationality itself**, and standards of justification and inquiry, are not universal, neutral, and tradition-independent (as the Enlightenment supposed), but are always situated *within* and constituted *by* particular **traditions** of thought and inquiry. This idea, developed especially in *Whose Justice? Which Rationality?* (1988), is fundamental to MacIntyre's critique of Enlightenment liberalism and his defense of a tradition-based approach to ethics and knowledge.

MacIntyre rejects the **Enlightenment ideal** of a single, universal, tradition-neutral rationality — the notion that there are objective standards of reason and justification, accessible to any rational person as such, independent of any particular historical or cultural context, by which all questions can be settled. This "view from nowhere," MacIntyre argues, is an illusion; the Enlightenment project of grounding morality and knowledge on such universal, tradition-free rational foundations has **failed**. There is no such neutral standpoint.

Instead, MacIntyre argues, all rational inquiry, all standards of justification, and all conceptions of justice and the virtues are **tradition-constituted**: they arise within, and are intelligible only in relation to, particular ongoing **traditions of inquiry** — coherent, historically-extended arguments and practices (such as the Aristotelian, Augustinian, Thomistic, or liberal traditions), each with its own conception of rationality, its own authoritative texts, and its own standards. Rationality is always the rationality *of* some tradition; there is no rationality *as such*. Yet — and this is crucial — MacIntyre's position is *not* a relativism in which traditions are simply incommensurable and beyond rational comparison. Traditions can enter into **rational encounter and conflict**; a tradition can face an "**epistemological crisis**" when it can no longer resolve its own problems by its own resources, and one tradition can be shown to be **rationally superior** to another if it can explain the failures and limitations of its rival better than the rival can explain its own — thus offering a genuine, if tradition-situated, form of rational progress. This subtle account of a rationality that is both tradition-bound and yet capable of rational comparison and progress is one of MacIntyre's most important and influential contributions.
ideas.Practices and internal goods:
The concepts of "**practices**" and their "**internal goods**" are central to Alasdair MacIntyre's influential attempt, in *After Virtue* (1981), to **reconstruct a coherent account of the virtues** — to give the concept of virtue a rational basis after the collapse of its traditional foundations. Together with the concepts of the "narrative unity of a human life" and "tradition," the notion of a practice forms the first stage of MacIntyre's definition of virtue.

MacIntyre defines a "**practice**" in a specific, technical sense: a **coherent, complex, socially established, cooperative human activity** through which "goods internal to that form of activity" are realized in the course of trying to achieve the standards of excellence appropriate to that activity — standards that partly define it. His examples include chess, architecture, farming, medicine, scientific inquiry, the arts, and games like football — activities that have their own internal standards of excellence, their own history, and their own traditions.

The crucial distinction is between **internal** and **external** goods. **External goods** are goods like money, status, power, and fame — goods that can be won through a practice but that are only *contingently* attached to it, that could be obtained in other ways, and that, when acquired, become the private possession of an individual (and are objects of competition). **Internal goods**, by contrast, are goods that can be achieved *only* by engaging in the practice itself and pursuing its standards of excellence — the specific excellences and satisfactions internal to chess-playing, or medicine, or painting, which cannot be obtained any other way and which, when achieved, are a good for the *whole community* who participate in the practice. To realize the internal goods of a practice, one must submit to its standards and develop the relevant excellences.

On this basis MacIntyre gives his first, partial definition of a **virtue**: a virtue is "an acquired human quality the possession and exercise of which tends to enable us to achieve those goods which are internal to practices." The virtues (justice, courage, honesty, and the rest) are precisely the qualities of character necessary to sustain practices and to attain their internal goods. This grounding of the virtues in the pursuit of the internal goods of practices is a key building block of MacIntyre's neo-Aristotelian ethics.
ideas.The fragmentation of moral language:
The "**fragmentation of moral language**" — the diagnosis that modern moral discourse consists of incoherent fragments of older, now-lost traditions — is the powerful and provocative starting point of Alasdair MacIntyre's *After Virtue* (1981), one of the most influential works of moral philosophy of the late twentieth century. It frames MacIntyre's sweeping critique of modern **liberal**, Enlightenment ethics and his call for a return to an Aristotelian, virtue-based tradition.

MacIntyre opens *After Virtue* with a striking and unsettling hypothesis. He asks us to imagine a **catastrophe** in which the natural sciences were destroyed — books burned, scientists killed — and then, later, people tried to reconstruct science from the surviving **fragments**: bits of theories, isolated experiments, half-remembered terms, all detached from the coherent theoretical framework that once gave them meaning. The result would be a discourse that *used* scientific language and *seemed* like science, but that was in fact incoherent, its terms wrenched from the context that made them intelligible. MacIntyre's provocative claim is that **this is precisely the state of moral language today**. Modern moral discourse consists of **surviving fragments** — words like "virtue," "duty," "rights," "good" — inherited from older, coherent moral traditions (above all the classical and Aristotelian tradition) but now torn from the context of shared beliefs about human nature, purpose (*telos*), and the good life that once gave them their sense.

The consequence, MacIntyre argues, is the characteristic **interminability and irresolvability of modern moral debate**: because we possess only fragments of incompatible moral schemes, with no shared framework to adjudicate them, our moral arguments (about abortion, war, justice, and the rest) reach no rational conclusion but degenerate into the mere assertion of rival, incommensurable positions. This condition, he argues, is the deep truth behind **emotivism** — the theory that moral judgments are merely expressions of feeling or attitude — which MacIntyre sees not as a correct account of morality *as such*, but as an accurate description of what morality has *become* after the failure of the Enlightenment project to provide a rational foundation for ethics. This diagnosis of moral fragmentation and disorder sets the stage for MacIntyre's argument that only a recovery of the Aristotelian tradition of the virtues, with its teleological conception of human life, can restore coherence and rationality to moral life.

works.After Virtue:
*After Virtue: A Study in Moral Theory* (1981) is Alasdair MacIntyre's masterpiece and one of the most important and influential works of moral philosophy of the late twentieth century — a powerful critique of modern **liberal**, Enlightenment ethics and an eloquent call for the recovery of an **Aristotelian**, virtue-based approach to morality. It is widely credited with reviving **virtue ethics** as a major force in contemporary moral philosophy and with launching the "communitarian" critique of liberal individualism.

The book opens with its famous and provocative diagnosis of the **fragmentation of modern moral language**. Through the "catastrophe" thought experiment, MacIntyre argues that modern moral discourse consists of incoherent **fragments** of older, now-lost moral traditions, wrenched from the frameworks that gave them meaning. The consequence is the characteristic **interminability** of modern moral debate — the failure of moral arguments to reach rational resolution — and the truth of **emotivism** (moral judgments as mere expressions of preference) as a description of what morality has *become*. MacIntyre traces this disorder to the **failure of the Enlightenment project** to provide a rational, tradition-independent foundation for morality — a failure he argues was inevitable, because in abandoning the older teleological framework (the Aristotelian conception of human beings as having a natural purpose or *telos*), the Enlightenment left morality without any coherent ground.

MacIntyre's constructive proposal is a return to the **Aristotelian tradition of the virtues**. He offers a rich, historically-informed reconstruction of the concept of virtue, built on three key ideas: the **internal goods of practices**, the **narrative unity of a human life**, and the moral **tradition** within which lives and practices are situated. Virtues, on this account, are the qualities of character that enable us to achieve the goods internal to practices, to pursue the good of a whole human life understood as a narrative quest, and to sustain the traditions that bear these goods. The book famously ends by contrasting the choice between "Nietzsche or Aristotle" and calling, in its closing lines, for the construction of new forms of community to sustain the moral life through a new "dark age" — awaiting "another, doubtless very different, St. Benedict." Profound and influential, *After Virtue* transformed contemporary ethics.
works.Whose Justice? Which Rationality?:
*Whose Justice? Which Rationality?* (1988) is Alasdair MacIntyre's important sequel to *After Virtue* — a major work in which he develops and defends his central thesis that both **justice** and **rationality** are **tradition-constituted**, that there is no single, universal, tradition-neutral standard of either, and that rational inquiry always proceeds from *within* particular traditions. It deepens and extends the argument of *After Virtue* and responds to the charge that his tradition-based approach collapses into relativism.

The book's very title poses its guiding questions: conceptions of **justice** and standards of **rationality** are always *someone's* and always *of some kind* — there is no conception of justice or rationality "as such," neutral among traditions. Against the **Enlightenment** dream of a universal, objective, tradition-independent rationality accessible to any rational person, MacIntyre argues that this ideal is an illusion that has demonstrably failed, and that all substantive conceptions of justice and practical rationality are embedded in, and intelligible only in relation to, particular ongoing **traditions of inquiry**.

MacIntyre pursues this thesis through detailed historical study of several rival traditions of justice and rationality across Western history — the **Aristotelian**, the **Augustinian**, the **Thomistic** (Aquinas's synthesis, which MacIntyre increasingly favors), the Scottish Enlightenment, and modern **liberalism** (which he provocatively argues is *itself* a tradition, despite its pretension to be the neutral, universal framework transcending all traditions). Each embodies a distinct conception of justice and its own standards of rational justification.

Crucially, MacIntyre argues that this position is **not relativism**. Traditions are not simply incommensurable and beyond rational comparison. A tradition can encounter an "**epistemological crisis**" when its own resources prove unable to resolve its problems; and one tradition can be shown **rationally superior** to another if, from within, it can better diagnose and explain the failures and limitations of its rival than the rival can explain its own — allowing genuine, if tradition-situated, rational progress and adjudication between traditions. Rich, learned, and rigorous, *Whose Justice? Which Rationality?* is the fullest statement of MacIntyre's mature philosophy of tradition-constituted rationality and justice.
